neighborhoods.name: The Hill District
primary_groups: African Americans from the Upper South (Virginia, the Carolinas)
neighborhoods.character: Pittsburgh's primary Black neighborhood, rising steeply above downtown. By the 1930s-1950s the Hill was a thriving center of jazz and Black culture — Wylie Avenue was compared to Harlem's Lenox Avenue. Urban renewal in the late 1950s demolished the Lower Hill to build the Civic Arena, displacing over 8,000 residents and destroying the commercial heart of the community.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Black and arrived in Pittsburgh, the Hill District was the neighborhood — Wylie Avenue was alive with jazz clubs and barbershops, and the Pittsburgh Courier was telling the story of Black America to a national audience.

neighborhoods.name: South Side / Birmingham
primary_groups: Ukrainian, Lithuanian, Polish, and Slovak immigrants
neighborhoods.character: Clustered along the Monongahela River in the shadow of the Jones & Laughlin steel mills. Workers lived in narrow row houses climbing steep hillsides. Each ethnic group built its own church — often an ornate structure out of proportion to the modest houses surrounding it. The neighborhood was defined by the rhythm of the mill shifts.
key_institutions: St. John Chrysostom Church (Slovak Byzantine Catholic)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The South Side was built in the shadow of the steel mills — if {ancestor_name} came from Eastern Europe and worked at J&L Steel, the row houses climbing the hillside above Carson Street were likely home, and the onion domes of the Byzantine churches marked the skyline.

neighborhoods.name: Bloomfield / Little Italy
primary_groups: Italian immigrants, predominantly from Abruzzo and Campania
neighborhoods.character: Pittsburgh's Italian neighborhood on Liberty Avenue east of downtown. Italian families built a self-contained community with groceries, bakeries, and social clubs. Unlike most urban Italian neighborhoods that eventually dispersed, Bloomfield retained its Italian identity well into the 21st century.
key_institutions: Italian Sons and Daughters of America
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Italian, Bloomfield was probably their neighborhood — Liberty Avenue was lined with Italian groceries and bakeries, and Immaculate Conception Church recorded the family's sacraments.

major_employers.name: Carnegie Steel / U.S. Steel — Homestead Works, Edgar Thomson Works
major_employers.wages: Skilled workers: $3-5/day (1890s-1900s). Common laborers: $1.25-1.75/day. Twelve-hour shifts, seven days a week, were standard until the 1923 reforms.
major_employers.working_conditions: The most dangerous industrial work in America. The Homestead Strike of 1892 — Pinkerton guards vs. steelworkers — was a defining moment in American labor history. The heat near open-hearth furnaces exceeded 130 degrees. Annual death rates in Pittsburgh steel mills averaged 1 in 200 workers in the early 1900s.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} worked steel in Pittsburgh, the conditions were brutal — twelve-hour shifts, seven days a week, in heat that melted shoe leather. The Pittsburgh Survey of 1907-1914 documented an industrial death rate that shocked the nation.

major_employers.name: Westinghouse Electric
major_employers.wages: Skilled electricians and machinists earned premium wages: $2.50-4.00/day (1900s). Westinghouse was considered a better employer than the steel mills.
major_employers.working_conditions: Cleaner and less dangerous than steelwork. George Westinghouse was considered a progressive employer for the era, introducing the half-Saturday holiday. The East Pittsburgh works employed up to 25,000 at its peak.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Westinghouse offered {ancestor_name} an alternative to the mills — the work was lighter, the hours shorter, and the factory in East Pittsburgh was building the electrical systems that would transform American life.

major_employers.name: H.J. Heinz Company
major_employers.wages: Lower than steelwork but steadier employment: $1.00-2.00/day (1900s). Heinz employed a high proportion of women.
major_employers.working_conditions: Henry Heinz was considered a model employer — his factories were clean by the standards of the day, and he provided employee dining rooms, swimming pools, and libraries. The workforce was heavily female and immigrant. Heinz's North Side factory was a major employer for neighborhood residents.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} — particularly a woman — needed factory work that did not involve the killing heat of a steel furnace, the Heinz plant on the North Side offered cleaner conditions and a boss who believed clean factories made better products.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Pittsburgh was an inland destination. Immigrants arrived via East Coast ports (primarily New York and Baltimore) and transferred by rail — the Pennsylvania Railroad and the Baltimore & Ohio were the primary routes into Pittsburgh.
port_of_entry_details.processing: No immigrant processing in Pittsburgh. Port-of-entry records are found at Ellis Island, Castle Garden, or the Port of Baltimore. Southern Black migrants arrived by rail and bus from Virginia, the Carolinas, and Georgia.
records_generated: Ship manifests at port of entry
Pennsylvania Railroad employment records (some at the Railroad Museum of Pennsylvania)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Boarding houses near the mills were the universal first stop for single men. Married families rented cramped row houses or wooden frame houses in mill neighborhoods. Company housing existed at some plants, particularly in satellite mill towns like Homestead and Braddock.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Extreme ethnic clustering organized around churches. Slovaks on the South Side, Poles in Polish Hill and Lawrenceville, Italians in Bloomfield, Black residents on the Hill, Irish in the Strip District. Each group's church was the anchor — often built before the surrounding houses.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Boarding house (single men, 6-24 months) to rental row house (families) to owned row house (10-20 years). Pittsburgh's hillside geography created isolated neighborhoods that reinforced ethnic clustering. Second-generation mobility moved families to suburban mill towns or outlying neighborhoods.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Pittsburgh's industrial economy depended on immigrant labor but native-born workers resented wage competition. Ethnic tensions were exploited by steel companies to prevent unionization — managers deliberately mixed ethnic groups in work crews to prevent communication and solidarity.
specific_incidents: 1877 Great Railroad Strike — Pittsburgh saw the worst violence, with 39 killed and Union Depot burned
1892 Homestead Strike — Pinkerton guards fired on strikers, 10 killed
1907 anti-Italian riots in several mill towns around Pittsburgh
nativist_organizations: KKK was active in Pittsburgh suburbs in the 1920s, targeting Catholics and immigrants

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. John Chrysostom Byzantine Catholic Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death registers in Church Slavonic and English. Byzantine Catholic parish records are held by the Archeparchy of Pittsburgh.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If the {surname} family was Slovak or Rusyn, St. John Chrysostom's registers may hold their sacramental records — the Byzantine Catholic churches on the South Side served communities that transplanted their Old World faith whole into the shadow of the steel mills.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: National Slovak Society
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership applications with birthplace, date of immigration, occupation, and family data. Insurance policy records. Headquartered in Pittsburgh.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The National Slovak Society was headquartered in Pittsburgh and its membership rolls may contain {ancestor_name}'s application — these fraternal organizations recorded biographical details that government records often missed.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Bethel AME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community on the Hill
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls and baptism records. The church served as a social center for Hill District families arriving from the South.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Bethel AME on the Hill was where arriving families from the South found a foothold — the church's membership rolls may record {ancestor_name}'s entry into Pittsburgh's Black community.

source_references.author: S.J. Kleinberg
source_references.title: The Shadow of the Mills: Working-Class Families in Pittsburgh, 1870-1907
source_references.notes: Uses census data and city records to reconstruct the daily life of steelworker families, covering wages, housing, diet, mortality, and women's economic contributions.

source_references.author: Laurence Glasco
source_references.title: The WPA History of the Negro in Pittsburgh
source_references.notes: Published edition of the 1940s WPA Federal Writers Project manuscript documenting Black community history in Pittsburgh, including the Hill District.

source_references.author: Paul Krause
source_references.title: The Battle for Homestead, 1880-1892: Politics, Culture, and Steel
source_references.notes: Detailed study of the Homestead Strike and the immigrant steelworker community, using company records, census data, and oral histories.
